title: Anthropic names Alibaba in largest known distillation attack; Mythos cracks classified systems; OpenAI builds its own chips
subhead: The AI power contest enters a new phase: Anthropic publicly accuses Alibaba of running 28.8 million queries through 25,000 fake accounts, its Mythos model finds vulnerabilities in classified US systems within hours, and OpenAI unveils its first custom chip to reduce dependence on Nvidia. Meanwhile, China detains Japanese nationals over rare earths, the G7 explores a 'trusted partners' framework for AI model access, and China retaliates with sanctions on 10 US defense firms.

sections.heading: Anthropic accuses Alibaba of largest known AI distillation attack
sections.body:
Anthropic sent a letter to the US Senate Banking Committee accusing Alibaba of carrying out 'the largest known distillation attack on Anthropic to date', as reported by CNBC and Bloomberg. The letter, addressed to Senators Tim Scott and Elizabeth Warren on June 10, claims operators affiliated with Alibaba and its AI lab used roughly 25,000 fraudulent accounts to run 28.8 million queries against Anthropic's models between April 22 and June 5. Distillation is a training method where a smaller model is built using outputs from a stronger one — effectively copying capabilities without permission.

Anthropic wrote that Alibaba 'ignored the Trump Administration's warnings', referring to an April White House Office of Science and Technology Policy memorandum pledging to help AI companies detect and coordinate against industrial-scale distillation. In February, Anthropic had identified three earlier distillation campaigns from DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax.

The accusation comes at a fraught moment for Anthropic: the company remains under a Trump administration export control directive, imposed June 12, that forced it to suspend access to its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models for all foreign nationals. Anthropic has sent executives to Washington for negotiations but has not said when the models will come back online.
sections.whyItMatters: The allegation puts a specific company and specific numbers behind what US AI labs have been warning about for months. If accurate, the scale — 28.8 million queries via 25,000 accounts — suggests a systematic, well-resourced effort, not opportunistic scraping. It also places Alibaba in an awkward position: the same day this story broke, Alibaba separately sued the Pentagon to remove its 'Chinese military company' designation.

sections.heading: Mythos finds classified system vulnerabilities within hours
sections.body:
Anthropic's Mythos AI model identified vulnerabilities in highly sensitive US government computer systems during a classified red-team testing exercise, according to an AP report confirmed by Reuters and The Next Web. A US official told the AP that Mythos found the flaws within hours, not weeks.

Senator Mark Warner, vice-chair of the Senate Intelligence Committee, said in a June 11 hearing that General Joshua Rudd, who leads the NSA and Cyber Command, told him Mythos 'broke into almost all of our classified systems, not in weeks, but in hours.' The testing was conducted under Project Glasswing, Anthropic's controlled-access program for vetted organizations, not an external intrusion. The UK's AI Security Institute has assessed Mythos as substantially more capable at cyber offence than any model it had previously tested.

The episode sits inside a deep contradiction: the NSA has been authorized to keep using Mythos on classified networks, while the same administration forced Anthropic to disable the model for all foreign access on June 12. Anthropic has called the reported jailbreak that triggered the shutdown 'minor vulnerabilities' that other publicly available models can also discover, and no harmful result from the disclosed jailbreaks has been reported.
sections.whyItMatters: Mythos demonstrated a real and measurable cyber-offensive capability in a controlled setting — enough to alarm the intelligence community. The contradiction of a model being too dangerous for allies to access but essential for the NSA to use is the policy knot the US government has not untied.

sections.heading: OpenAI unveils first custom chip 'Jalapeño' with Broadcom
sections.body:
OpenAI unveiled Jalapeño, its first custom AI inference chip developed in partnership with Broadcom, as reported by Reuters and Gizmodo. The chip is an ASIC — not a general-purpose GPU — designed to run models faster and cheaper. OpenAI said the development cycle took nine months, which it called 'the fastest ASIC development cycle ever achieved in high-performance advanced semiconductors.'

Broadcom CEO Hock Tan said the companies plan to implement Jalapeño chips into full-scale production data centers with Microsoft and other partners beginning in 2026. OpenAI and Broadcom had announced plans in October to build enough custom chip racks to supply ten gigawatts of power. OpenAI president Greg Brockman said the in-house chip supply would deliver cheaper products and minimize wait times during demand spikes.

The move reduces OpenAI's dependence on Nvidia, which currently supplies most of its silicon, though OpenAI also maintains partnerships with AMD, Amazon, and Cerebras. The chip itself was partly designed using OpenAI's own AI systems — a concrete example of the recursive self-improvement the company has discussed in its safety arguments.
sections.whyItMatters: OpenAI becoming a chip designer — even in partnership — shifts the AI infrastructure stack. If Jalapeño delivers on cost and performance, it pressures Nvidia's pricing power and pushes other frontier labs toward vertical integration. The nine-month design cycle, partly AI-assisted, also sets a new benchmark for chip development speed.

sections.heading: China detains Japanese nationals over rare earths as mineral warfare escalates
sections.body:
China has detained two Japanese nationals in the northeastern port city of Dalian on suspicion of violating laws against smuggling prohibited imports or exports, according to Japan's Chief Cabinet Secretary Minoru Kihara. Japanese media, including the Asahi Shimbun, reported that at least one detainee was an employee of a major Japanese electrical machinery manufacturer, suspected of attempting to export processed products containing rare earth elements from China.

Chinese customs data showed exports to Japan of several rare earth materials used in high-performance magnets remained negligible in May, as UPI reported. China tightened export controls on seven categories of medium and heavy rare earth items in April 2025, and the detentions mark an escalation from supply disruption to personnel risk for foreign companies operating in China.

The detentions came alongside broader retaliation: China imposed sanctions on 10 US military-linked companies, including drone makers and defense contractors, calling it a response to 'wrongful' US actions. Japan's Prime Minister Takaichi, meanwhile, used the G7 summit to propose a Joint Critical Minerals Stockpiling Framework among G7 nations. G7 leaders agreed to reduce dependence on any single non-G7 supplier for rare earths and permanent magnets to below 60% by 2030.
sections.whyItMatters: The detentions move China's rare earth leverage beyond export quotas and customs delays into direct legal risk for foreign employees. That changes the calculus for Japanese and Korean firms with operations in China — and accelerates the G7 push for alternative supply chains.

sections.heading: G7 explores 'trusted partners' AI access framework amid Anthropic restrictions
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Évian-les-Bains, leaders discussed a proposal to give selected 'trusted partners' access to advanced US AI models like Anthropic's Mythos, according to the EconoTimes and the Financial Times. The discussions, which took place during meetings with US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, were a direct response to the June 12 export control directive that forced Anthropic to suspend all foreign access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5.

Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei and Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis both called for a US-led AI coalition at a closed-door lunch with G7 leaders and tech executives, CNBC reported. Amodei proposed international cooperation on structured access to frontier models and chip trade that excludes China. Canadian PM Mark Carney agreed the US could lead such a coalition. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman called for 'an international forum for discussion that establishes globally accepted standards for testing.'

Before the restrictions, Anthropic had provided Mythos access to organizations in more than 15 countries, including entities in healthcare, communications, energy, and water infrastructure. The EU is also seeking access to the model to assess its capabilities and risks.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is trying to build a framework that balances the security concerns driving export controls with the allied access those same controls just revoked. The outcome will determine whether AI model access becomes a treaty-grade alliance issue — like nuclear technology — or stays in ad hoc regulatory limbo.

sections.heading: Alibaba sues Pentagon over military designation while US holds off on DeepSeek blacklist
sections.body:
Alibaba filed a federal lawsuit against the US Department of Defense demanding removal from its list of 'Chinese military companies', as reported by Reuters, the New York Times, and AP. Alibaba argued it has no ties to China's military and that the government violated the law by making the designation without sufficient evidence.

The lawsuit landed the same day Anthropic publicly accused Alibaba of running the largest known distillation attack on its AI models — creating a stark juxtaposition. Alibaba is simultaneously fighting to be treated as a legitimate commercial actor in US courts while being accused of systematically extracting rival AI capabilities through fraud.

Separately, Reuters reported that the Trump administration has held off adding DeepSeek, memory chipmaker CXMT, and more than 100 other Chinese firms to the Commerce Department's Entity List despite an interagency panel approving the listings last year. The delay is intended to avoid escalating tensions with Beijing, according to sources familiar with the matter. A senior State Department official said DeepSeek had supported China's military and intelligence operations and attempted to use Southeast Asian shell companies to obtain advanced US chips illegally.
sections.whyItMatters: The US is simultaneously tightening and loosening its grip on Chinese tech: Alibaba gets a formal military designation but sues to challenge it, DeepSeek gets cleared for blacklisting but the politics won't let it happen. The space between what the bureaucracy recommends and what the White House executes is where US-China tech policy actually lives right now.

bottomLine: June 25 crystallized the central tension in AI geopolitics: the technology is simultaneously too powerful to share and too powerful to withhold. Anthropic's Mythos found classified US system vulnerabilities in hours — proving its offensive capability — while Anthropic accused Alibaba of the largest known distillation attack, and Alibaba turned around and sued the Pentagon. OpenAI's Jalapeño chip, developed in nine months with AI assistance, shows frontier labs building their own silicon to escape Nvidia dependency. The G7's 'trusted partners' framework is an attempt to resolve the access paradox, but it remains a proposal, not a policy. Meanwhile, China's detention of Japanese nationals over rare earths and sanctions on 10 US firms show Beijing escalating from export controls to personnel risk and retaliatory blacklists. The technology is moving faster than any of the institutions trying to govern it.
sources: CNBC, Reuters, AP, The Next Web, Bloomberg, Gizmodo, UPI, South China Morning Post, EconoTimes, Financial Times, Reason, The Diplomat, Yahoo News, MSN, Firstpost, DiGiTimes, Asahi Shimbun, OpenAI, Anthropic
